html:
LPKF is not the hidden net-cash royalty story in the prior draft. The current filings do not support that. The better thesis is more aggressive and cleaner: this is a scarce, listed European pure play on glass-substrate advanced packaging, temporarily buried inside a loss-making specialty laser equipment company.

At about €22.05 per share, or roughly CHF20.1, LPKF has a market cap near €540 million and essentially the same enterprise value. That is not cheap on 2026 numbers. Q1 revenue fell 32% to €17.1 million, adjusted EBIT was -€5.7 million, and free cash flow was -€7.6 million. 2026 guidance is only €105-120 million of revenue with -3.0% to 4.5% adjusted EBIT margin, and management explicitly excludes potential advanced-packaging volume orders from that forecast in its Q1 report.

That ugly present is exactly why the setup still works. The reported business is at a cyclical and restructuring trough, while the value-creating variable is off-consensus: whether LIDE becomes a required process step for glass-core substrates and glass interposers used in AI, HPC, chiplets, CPO, and large advanced packages.

The market is now partially awake to this, but still has not modeled the shape of the payoff. LPKF is being treated like a hyped machinery stock after a 2026 retail/social-media squeeze. That is directionally fair, since the stock is up massively and Warburg correctly warned that the “glass narrative” had run ahead of the order book. But the important point is that the order book has not yet had a chance to show the real option. LPKF says it is in concrete discussions with several customers for initial production systems, LIDE is already being used by semiconductor customers in test and R&D, and expected follow-on orders would mark the transition toward series production.

The variant view is that LPKF is not selling a few niche tools. It is trying to become a process stack supplier for glass packaging: TGV formation through LIDE, glass package singulation, laser bonding of multilayer glass stacks, Tensor ablation, and eventually co-packaged optics integration. In an April interview with The Elec, CEO Klaus Fiedler said mass production of glass substrates could begin in 2027 and that more than 80% of major global players had selected LPKF equipment for validation, learning, and scaling. Even if that statement is partly commercial positioning, it is too material to ignore.

The reason this matters is that glass substrates are no longer a science project. Intel has publicly framed glass as a way to overcome organic substrate limits for AI and data-center packaging, and TrendForce has reported that Intel, TSMC, Samsung, Rapidus, and SK Absolics are targeting mass-production windows around 2027-2028. The bottleneck is not “does glass have better material properties?” It is yield, process reliability, downstream qualification, and panel-level economics. That is where LPKF’s value sits.

On consensus, the stock is expensive: roughly 35x 2028 EPS using older sell-side numbers. On a glass ramp model, it becomes cheap quickly. If the legacy core recovers to €130-150 million of revenue at a 10-12% EBIT margin, that is €13-18 million of EBIT. Add €60-80 million of advanced-packaging revenue at 25-35% segment EBIT margin, and group EBIT becomes €30-45 million. At today’s EV, that is 12-18x forward EBIT for a company with scarce process IP in a strategic AI packaging transition. If advanced packaging reaches low-triple-digit revenue, the current EV is closer to 8-10x plausible 2028-2029 EBIT.

The core business is not worthless either. Development has defense/R&D support, Electronics has PCB laser-cutting growth, Welding is being moved away from weak automotive dependence toward smart robotics, medical, and consumer electronics, and Solar is depressed by the perovskite transition rather than permanently dead. North Star is a real cost program, not just a slogan: production consolidation, headcount reduction, and a 2028 double-digit EBIT margin target. The balance sheet is adequate, with a 67.8% equity ratio and a syndicated facility extended to 2028, but it is not a fortress. Cash was only €5.8 million at Q1, so a prolonged delay would matter.

The biggest risk is timing. If glass substrates remain pilot-line only until 2029-2030, the stock can be cut in half because the current price already embeds a meaningful technology premium. Competition from Philoptics, TRUMPF/Schmid, Chinese suppliers, and customer in-sourcing also matters. The thesis breaks if LPKF fails to convert validation tools into production orders by 2027, if major AI customers stay fully on organic substrates and CoWoS-style scaling, or if liquidity tightens before orders arrive.

My view: this is no longer a sleepy value stock, but it is still one of the better asymmetric small-cap AI infrastructure plays because the consensus model is anchored to the broken 2026 P&L while the real variable is whether LPKF becomes a tollbooth in glass advanced packaging.

Price targets from today’s €22.05, approximately CHF20.1:

Bear: €12, about CHF10.9, -46%. No material LIDE production orders, 2026 losses persist, and the stock rerates back toward a normal specialty-equipment multiple.

Base: €35, about CHF31.9, +59%. Initial production orders arrive, 2027 revenue visibility improves, North Star gets the core back toward profitability, and the market begins valuing LPKF as a strategic glass-packaging equipment supplier.

Bull: €65, about CHF59.3, +195%. Glass substrates move into broad 2027-2028 production planning, LPKF wins multiple production lines across TGV and adjacent glass-processing steps, and investors underwrite €50 million-plus medium-term EBIT power.
